critic_raw: The multi-task learning experiment for 30-day readmission prediction is methodologically careful in several dimensions, but has one central concern: the confound isolation between multi-task learning and text modality. The experiment pits a multi-task model combining structured EHR data and clinical notes against a logistic regression baseline trained on structured data only. This design conflates two orthogonal hypotheses: (1) whether multi-task learning (joint training with auxiliary objectives) improves the primary task versus single-task training on the same features, and (2) whether adding clinical notes as a feature improves performance over structured-only models. If the multi-task model outperforms the baseline, it could be because clinical notes provide unique signal, because the auxiliary tasks regularize the representation, or because the combined model has strictly more information than the baseline. The experiment cannot attribute the improvement to multi-task learning specifically. A three-arm design — structured-only MTL, notes+structured single-task, notes+structured MTL — would be needed to isolate these contributions.
all_issues_raised: Multi-task learning and text modality contributions are confounded: the proposed model adds both MTL and clinical notes simultaneously versus a structured-only baseline, making it impossible to attribute any performance gain to the MTL architecture versus the additional modality.

round_transcripts.critic: The multi-task learning experiment for 30-day readmission prediction is methodologically careful in several dimensions, but has one central concern: the confound isolation between multi-task learning and text modality. The experiment pits a multi-task model combining structured EHR data and clinical notes against a logistic regression baseline trained on structured data only. This design conflates two orthogonal hypotheses: (1) whether multi-task learning (joint training with auxiliary objectives) improves the primary task versus single-task training on the same features, and (2) whether adding clinical notes as a feature improves performance over structured-only models. If the multi-task model outperforms the baseline, it could be because clinical notes provide unique signal, because the auxiliary tasks regularize the representation, or because the combined model has strictly more information than the baseline. The experiment cannot attribute the improvement to multi-task learning specifically. A three-arm design — structured-only MTL, notes+structured single-task, notes+structured MTL — would be needed to isolate these contributions.
round_transcripts.defender: The confound critique is valid as an aspiration for complete mechanistic attribution, but mischaracterizes the stated hypothesis. The paper's hypothesis is that 'a multi-task learning model utilizing joint embedding of clinical notes and structured EHR data predicts 30-day readmission more accurately than a traditional separate modeling approach.' The hypothesis explicitly bundles the multi-task architecture and clinical notes together as the treatment, with the structured-only logistic regression baseline representing the traditional approach. This is a clinically motivated comparison — practitioners want to know whether the full proposed system (joint embedding with MTL) outperforms current practice, not whether each component contributes independently. The design directly tests the stated hypothesis. The three-arm ablation the critic requests would answer a different, more mechanistic question. It would be an appropriate future-work extension, but its absence does not invalidate the comparison of the proposed system against its intended baseline.
